item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that were used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as no source found indicates the first image has been released under Creative Commons, and the second image comes from here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the images have been removed.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
So the standard is back to freely cross-referencing relevant SCPs?

Well, within reason, and if done well. We don't need a fish article that is crosslinked to all articles about anomalous fish simply because they're all fish. We have a Crosslinks Guide that explains how to tell.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Stumbled across this while I was looking at old comments of mine. So the standard is back to freely cross-referencing relevant SCPs? This article was made when the Fishmonger incident and its incredibly destructive effect on the initial Foundation canon was still fairly fresh in the minds of authors and staff, so cross-referencing SCPs you didn't write was frowned upon and SCP blackboxing was an acceptable workaround for that, among other things.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright, so we have a blackboxed skip here. This blackboxed skip needs to be something that A) is a shapeshifter and B) has blood that can be extracted for use in the prep procedures for SCP-1961. The instances of SCP-2408-1 fit that bill. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-1961 is a 2.5 m x 1.5 m x 1.5 m mirrored booth, composed of wood, steel, and [DATA EXPUNGED]. It is designed to emit a mixture of ████████ and ██████ radiation and concentrate the rays on a human subject, previously injected with [DATA EXPUNGED] and processed SCP-███ blood. [See Document 1961-P-3 for synthesis and administration instructions.] Use of SCP-1961 is fatal to subjects who have not been prepared with the appropriate injections. SCP-1961 processing is not reversible.

To this:

SCP-1961 is a 2.5 m x 1.5 m x 1.5 m mirrored booth, composed of wood, steel, and [DATA EXPUNGED]. It is designed to emit a mixture of ████████ and ██████ radiation and concentrate the rays on a human subject, previously injected with [DATA EXPUNGED] and processed SCP-2408-1 blood. [See Document 1961-P-3 for synthesis and administration instructions.] Use of SCP-1961 is fatal to subjects who have not been prepared with the appropriate injections. SCP-1961 processing is not reversible.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was just going to make a Ditto joke (surprised no one has yet) But something at the end caught me. Should a note to O5 have marked out lines? If this ever became part of a lower clearance file, then maybe. Given the content though, and what I know about O5, this would never end up in a file, so why the marking out?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: However, there's no clear indication of whether he's right, or just unduly paranoid due to the obviously posthuman nature of the creations and all the stories in which posthumans (be they cyborgs, mutants, sapient robots that blur the standards of "person", or whatever) overthrow humanity. As written, he could be completely misguided and the transformed entities, though strange and secretive about certain aspects of themselves, could be entirely harmless to humanity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is not entirely unintentional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of the history of the elder things and shoggoths from At the Mountains of Madness - adaptive blob servants that will rise up to destroy their creators and masters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot. The -2s It reminded me of the physical manifestations of the Unclean from SCP-093. What I don't like is the note at the end. It reminded me of the classic "robots that we made which will destroy us" and it feels clichéd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You are correct. Some of the fine work of Wilhelm Reich, voted "The person most likely, after Nikola Tesla, to have actually built an SCP."

+  You have to admit, he looks like he built an SCP...
- OK, enough bad hair for one day!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Orgone energy accumulator.

(You didn't say we couldn't use GIS.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looks like a shower stall to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it a… porta-potty?

I have no idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Keep guessing. It is not an isolation booth, auditory testing booth, or a gas chamber.
Although I like the idea that I might use a gas chamber as an SCP illustration…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was going to say audiometry/hearing-test booth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was going to guess some kind of

Spoilers
gas chamber.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Isn't that one of those chambers used for psychological testing of isolation in a small space?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You will win a beer/cookie/Internet/whatever if you correctly guess what the item in the SCP's picture actually is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
OK, you gave me a good idea:

I edited the Addendum a bit to make the dates when the SCP was created and then taken under Foundation control indeterminate.

If you want to think all of it happened recently, that's cool. If you want to think that this was developed in 1950 and the Foundation grabbed it in 1952, you can read it that way. You could even decide it was developed in 1950 and some other group had it for many years for some reason.

So if the issue of the Foundation helping develop this thing bugs you, you have an out that is not inconsistent with the way the SCP is written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like to think that in the "present" coordination with other groups to this degree would be prohibited. But 20 years ago what was the policy like? 50? Whenever there is something like this that violates me current headcanon, I assume that it was a previous policy that is no longer supported.

That way I can enjoy more articles and tales without worrying about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you let lemurs into your SCPs, you can never get them to leave.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the "if you try to make an SCP it never works out well" vibe this is. It adds to the mystery of the Factory and friends, too, since they seem able to do it decently.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is now head-canon for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meh. It lacks lemurs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good edit. You may want to make it so that the (non-existent) document is censored, otherwise it just looks like you've left it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe the Foundation eventually dumps them into the Delta Quadrant?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, okay. I read that and just wondered how in the hell they figured out that that was necessary. Also makes me wonder if different treatments would end up with different effects, or if this is all the chamber's capable of doing.

I will say, the time limit definitely keeps this from being overly useful. Most effective, in my opinion, are the sense of superiority from turned subjects and the whole joining together deal. Loss of individuality is a real trigger for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup, upvoted. I still think that it can be improved to flow more naturally, but at least there are no sentences that stand out on their own. "indefinite period" sentence works much better in its new place, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, originally this reminded me of the "Vita-Rays" thing that was part of Captain America's origin. With further details, it no longer does so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm…kind of, a bit (thank you, Wikipedia). I swear I have not seen a single episode of DS 9, and the resemblance is unintentional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edited a bit to remove a [DATA EXPUNGED] and make the process read a bit more clearly — there is entire (non-existent) document explaining how to process a subject; it's not simple at all; I more wanted to imply "complicated to the point that you don't really want to know unless you're the one doing it."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So…it makes Odo from Deep Space 9?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a good SCP. Some minor points, though, mostly to do with this paragraph:

It is designed to emit a mixture of ████████ and ██████ radiation and concentrate the rays on a human subject, previously injected with [DATA EXPUNGED], [DATA EXPUNGED], and SCP-███ blood. The intent of the procedure is to give the treated subject the power to modify his/her physical form at will, and maintain the change voluntarily for an indefinite period.

- "Data" is plural; you only need to write "Data Expunged" once.
- The idea of a purposefully-designed SCP sits badly with me, although I realise this opinion is not universal.
- Simply injecting the subject with the blood of another SCP seems overly simplistic. Unless SCP-███ is an existing SCP specifically characterised by having blood that can somehow transform its subject, or at the very least not cause some kind of horrible reaction (as happens when you give someone the wrong blood type, for example) I'd make it so that the blood needed to undergo some kind of processing first. Otherwise it just starts sounding like alchemy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Update: Removed the lines you suggested I remove. Less cheesy now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There may have been some wish-fulfillment in what the developers originally wanted, but I don't think there is much in what they ended up getting. Having to spend most of your life as a blob of goop seems like a pretty high price for sometimes being able to change your form.

Yes, the subjects have to be pre-treated. The idea is the serum they're injected with was developed from things that could naturally change their form…like SCP-███.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is this really necessary? Removing this sentence from its paragraph does not impede the understanding of the skip's effect, but improves the overall tone of the article.

I threw this in for the folks who were expecting a mini-682. You're right, it's not essential to understanding the article, and I could remove it.

Inconsistency, should be fixed.

You've misunderstood. The first line refers to what the designers of the SCP wanted — a human who could change form indefinitely. The second line refers to what actually happens when one uses the SCP. It's a bit from my personal canon, that when people try to design an SCP, it NEVER works as intended.

but those two particular sentences strike me as cheesy.

You may be right. Maybe I don't need them to demonstrate that the researcher is upset over how the project has developed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. That's why they always say "there is no canon" around here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's how it goes in my version of the (non) canon:

The Foundation is not quite as secret as it would like to be. It's known at high levels of the military and some other government organizations.

I completely respect your interpretation of the canon, and I know I not only couldn't, but shouldn't make you change anything, but I disagree. I agree to disagree.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a certain wish-fulfillment vibe to the basic concept, but this actually turned out pretty good! One question: the subjects have to first be treated (including injection of SCP-███ blood) before going into the chamber?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the spirit of this article, but the execution could use some work. Some phrases that especially bother me:

(one D-Class previously assigned to SCP-███ clean-up duty unsuccessfully attempted to escape by simulating SCP-███ and attacking his guards)

Is this really necessary? Removing this sentence from its paragraph does not impede the understanding of the skip's effect, but improves the overall tone of the article.

and maintain the change voluntarily for an indefinite period.

Form conversion is physically tiring, and SCP-1961-1 subjects have not been able to retain human form for a period longer than four hours

Inconsistency, should be fixed.

They are a new, alien, life-form that considers itself superior to us.
They will be our masters, not our servants.

I like the O5 note, and think that it's a good final touch for an already good idea, but those two particular sentences strike me as cheesy.

Once again, I don't think that the concept needs any changes at all. This could well be the best thing that can be done to the transformation mirror idea. But the execution just not as good as the concept. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's how it goes in my version of the (non) canon:

The Foundation is not quite as secret as it would like to be. It's known at high levels of the military and some other government organizations.

It doesn't normally cooperate in this way with other organizations, but the O5's can pretty much do what they want, and are not always exempt from politics. One way to think of this SCP is as a rogue project sponsored by one or two O5's, that the rest choose to ignore. This is not a good thing; it's asking for trouble; in fact, I'm presenting this SCP as an example of why it might not be good for the Foundation to act as R & D for other organizations. The Foundation wants to contain the stuff; the other organizations want to use it.

The Foundation is keeping a population of -1's and -2's around for experiments…to figure out their capabilities and how to defend against them…and they're pack rats in general. It's the same reason they keep all sorts of other dangerous living SCPs around. Most of them are former D-class, and as such are not involved in operations. The Foundation is very suspicious of the couple of non-D-class converted (a hint that there is something not quite right about this SCP's effects).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When and why was a "general" involved? It completely breaks what little of a collective canon we have, how essentially the Foundation is secret to any other organizations to the greatest extent possible, let alone any militaries (implied by the last note).

First off, as oft-stated, there is no canon per se. That said, most articles/tales that address the Foundation's dealings with external forces and organizations have it acknowledged, however quietly, by a variety of world governments and militaries. The US, Soviet, Nazi, Chinese, British, Iranian, and Canadian governments come to mind off the top of my head. No formal and definitive origin story has ever been definitively established for the Foundation, though several possible and mutually contradictory stories do exist. In at least some of them, the Foundation was originally created as a partially military organization. Similarly, the GOC is often shown as being in league with governments. So, I think it probably safe to assume (at least major) governments are aware of the Foundation, though the general population most certainly would not be.

Second of all, it's been established that some parts of the Foundation itself are structured militarily. At least one General (Bowe) has been established as being part of the Foundation. I don't think this is what the author was meaning to imply here, but it's worth noting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanted to suggest that it's a bad idea for the Foundation to get involved in black-ops research, but that it happens occasionally anyway. Officially, it's not supposed to happen, but the O5s are not entirely above politics and owing someone a favor now and then. It nearly always creates a conflict where the Foundation wants to keep the product of the research contained, while some other organization thinks it's useful and wants to get their hands on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. The note is a logical progression from the feelings of the person running the Site, and his concerns about the potential outcome should this SCP be left unchecked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why exactly did the Foundation collaborate with ANYONE in assisting to create a potential SCP from products/parts of any other held SCP? When and why was a "general" involved? It completely breaks what little of a collective canon we have, how essentially the Foundation is secret to any other organizations to the greatest extent possible, let alone any militaries (implied by the last note). Why would any object with an SCP designation be "in dispute" for whether or not it should be handed to other organizations? Also, why should the human byproducts of the SCP be allowed to participate in official Foundation activities? Why are they being let to live beyond specific study purposes, rather than being destroyed as per normal Foundation procedure? I really don't like any of the article past the description's explanation of the relationship between -1s and -2s.

Until these are answered or resolved to my liking, I'm keeping this at a downvote.

This article isn't without merit, I like the core element of it. However, something about the last part feels OOC and the aliens seem a little bit corny (thinking of the Darloks from MoO) and frankly unrealistic to the canon as it stands.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1961 was developed in ████ by the █████████ Research Group for the ███, with semi-official access to certain Foundation materials.

I'm bothered by the idea that the Foundation would give non-Foundation people access to SCPs or Foundation information. As an alternative, maybe a U.S. military black-ops organization had its own mini-Foundation which created SCP-1961, and when the Foundation found out it seized everything the mini-Foundation had.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For what it's worth (which ain't much, I'll freely admit), I really like that final note. The researcher's recommendation that the entire project be scrapped, despite the fact that SCP does not stand for "Secure, Contain, Obliterate"; the idea that even after the cleanup a while back, there's still military interest in what the Foundation can do; the simple fact that researchers, even after all they do, are still, at base, human enough to fight the production of basically post-human creatures - it all just rings right for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great job dude. Now we wont have to see one of these crash and burn every couple of months.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair. And just to clarify, the Kandra comparison is mostly superficial, and certainly not a complaint- it just reminded me a bit of something I liked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a lot I like about this concept, but the article has a fair ammount of fat to trim, and something is bothering me about that end note. With as much information as > there is to cover, the length will be understandably hard to control; however, if you can pare it down and do something for the pacing / flow, I can easily see myself
upvoting.

I agree with you that I had to include a lot of information and it felt a bit clunky to me when it was all down. (As an aside: If someone reading this likes the concept and feels like they can tighten this up, PM me please.) I added the end note to elaborate on the idea that this was developed as a joint project involving the Foundation, and others may be more enthusiastic about it than the Foundation researchers who have seen it close-up. Maybe there is a better way to do that?

Aside: I'm somewhat reminded of the Kandra from the Mistborn trilogy. Based on the wetsuits it seems that an exoskeleton helps the -1 instances out quite a bit; it
wouldn't be as secure, but could the same effect be achieved by providing them an endoskeleton to wrap around?

I didn't have anything in mind from another canon when writing this, but am not surprised that something like it would be out there. An endoskeleton would work…I chose the containment suits over an endoskeleton for the (unstated) reason that it hides the form of the -1s that can't transform. It also keeps them from merging into -2s when the researchers don't want them doing that.

Likewise, given that there are no longer distinctions between types of tissue, is there anything to prevent -1 instances from eatting to excess in order to increase
their usable mass? Or do they even eat?

I thought about adding something on this point, but didn't want to make the SCP even longer. I was thinking of saying that -1s eat enough to maintain their mass, but have a high enough metabolism to not increase it. -2s, on the other hand, can grow through excessive food. I'd like to keep the -1s human-sized, while the -2s can grow indefinitely large.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a lot I like about this concept, but the article has a fair ammount of fat to trim, and something is bothering me about that end note. With as much information as there is to cover, the length will be understandably hard to control; however, if you can pare it down and do something for the pacing / flow, I can easily see myself upvoting.

Aside: I'm somewhat reminded of the Kandra from the Mistborn trilogy. Based on the wetsuits it seems that an exoskeleton helps the -1 instances out quite a bit; it wouldn't be as secure, but could the same effect be achieved by providing them an endoskeleton to wrap around?

Likewise, given that there are no longer distinctions between types of tissue, is there anything to prevent -1 instances from eatting to excess in order to increase their usable mass? Or do they even eat?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is what the "Transformation Mirror" transformed into by the time I was done with it.
